s: Bangkok's oldest English library, born from a ladies' reading circle
w: Bangkok's English-language bookworms have gathered here since 1869, when a group of expatriate women founded the Bangkok Ladies' Library Association. The neoclassical domed rotunda was commissioned in 1921 by American doctor T. Heyward Hays, in memory of his late wife Jennie, who chaired the board. Italian architects Mario Tamagno and Giovanni Ferrero designed it. A quietly exquisite room.
_facts: Founded in 1869 as the Bangkok Ladies' Library Association
Current building commissioned in 1921 by Dr T. Heyward Hays in memory of his wife Jennie Neilson Hays
Designed by Italian architects Mario Tamagno and Giovanni Ferrero in neoclassical style
Completed in 1922; received ASA Architectural Conservation Award in 1982
Registered as an ancient monument in 2001

s: A Borneo trader's 1837 mosque now clad in neoclassical stucco
w: Musa Bafadel, an Indonesian-Arab merchant from Pontianak, Borneo, built this mosque in wood on the riverbank in 1837 and named it after his son. King Rama V had the community relocated inland in 1899 when the government built a customs house on the pier site. The wooden structure was replaced in 1934 with the present two-storey building: Romanesque pillars, neoclassical floral motifs, wooden shutters -- a mosque that looks borrowed from a Rangoon shophouse.
_facts: Founded in 1837 by Musa Bafadel, an Indonesian-Arab trader from Pontianak, Borneo, and named after his son Haroon
Originally built in wood on the banks of the Chao Phraya River
Relocated about 500 metres inland in 1899 during the reign of King Rama V
Present two-storey building completed in 1934, influenced by European townhouse design with neoclassical floral motifs and Romanesque pillars

s: A Buddhist temple built in the shape of a Chinese junk
w: King Rama III, unnerved by the spread of Western steamships in Thai waters, commissioned this temple's main viharn in the shape of a Chinese junk in the 1830s -- reasoning that a future generation might never see a real one. The replica hull is four storeys of prayer space, complete with masts, rudder, and a cannon-shaped finial at the prow. A temple that doubles as a ship that never sailed.
_facts: Temple dates to the Ayutthaya era
The junk-shaped viharn was ordered by King Rama III to preserve knowledge of Chinese junk design as Western steamships spread
The structure resembles a Chinese sailing vessel with masts and rudder
Known in English as 'the boat temple'
Additional buildings enshrine a Buddha relic from Sri Lanka and the Goddess of Mercy

s: Tamil Hindu temple built by colonialism's refugees in 1879
w: After India became a British colony in 1858, a group of Tamil Hindus from the south chose Bangkok over life under the Crown. Their leader, Vaithi Padayatchi, built this Dravidian-style temple on Silom Road in 1879 -- its towering gopuram rising from a Bangkok lane thick with marigold-garland sellers. The main Tamil Hindu temple in Thailand, and a monument to emigration as resistance. Locals still call it Wat Khaek.
_facts: Built in 1879 by Vaithi Padayatchi, a Tamil Hindu immigrant
Founded by a Tamil community that preferred Bangkok to life under British colonial rule after 1858
South Indian Dravidian architecture style with a gopuram tower
Located in Bang Rak district at corner of Silom and Pan roads
The main Tamil Hindu temple in Thailand; locally known as Wat Khaek

s: Bangkok's first true cinema, bankrolled by a king who loved movies
w: King Prajadhipok spent 9,000,000 baht of his personal funds on this Modernist picture palace, opened in July 1933 to mark the 150th anniversary of Bangkok's founding. He chose a Thai architect, Mom Chao Samaichaloem Kridakorn, just weeks out of Beaux-Arts school -- a conspicuously bold call when European architects held every major brief. Today it hosts khon masked dance, but the soaring Deco auditorium survives intact.
_facts: Opened 3 July 1933, commissioned by King Prajadhipok as part of Bangkok's 150th anniversary celebrations
King invested 9,000,000 baht of personal funds
Designed by Mom Chao Samaichaloem Kridakorn, recently graduated in Beaux-Arts Architecture
Built by the Bangkok Dock Company; Crown Property Bureau currently owns the building
Now hosts performances of classical Thai khon dance after years as a cinema

s: Field Marshal Phibun's Arc de Triomphe, built on cleared shophouses
w: Field Marshal Plaek Phibunsongkhram envisaged this as Bangkok's Arc de Triomphe, with Ratchadamnoen Avenue as the Champs-Elysees -- never mind that the residents, mostly Thai-Chinese shopkeepers, were evicted with 60 days' notice and hundreds of shade trees felled. Commissioned in 1939 to mark the 1932 constitutional revolution, its bas-reliefs were carved by the Italian sculptor Corrado Feroci, who had already made half the monuments in the city. Scene of every major Thai protest since.
_facts: Commissioned by Field Marshal Plaek Phibunsongkhram in 1939 to commemorate the 1932 Siamese revolution
Phibun explicitly modelled the boulevard and monument on the Champs-Elysees and Arc de Triomphe
Local residents (mostly Thai-Chinese) were evicted with 60 days' notice; hundreds of shade trees were cut down
Bas-reliefs executed by Italian sculptor Corrado Feroci (Thai name: Silpa Bhirasri)
Completed 24 June 1940; has been the focal point of every major Thai protest since 1973

s: The monument the sculptor called 'the victory of embarrassment'
w: Erected in 1941 to celebrate Thailand's brief, semi-forgotten war against Vichy French Indochina -- a 1940 conflict that netted some Cambodian and Lao territory before international pressure stopped the fighting. The five 'heroic realism' statues were made by the same Italian sculptor Corrado Feroci who did the Democracy Monument, but he disliked how his work was combined with the central obelisk and reportedly called it 'the victory of embarrassment.' Names of 656 dead are inscribed on the shaft.
_facts: Erected June 1941 to commemorate the Franco-Thai War of 1940-41, in which Thailand fought Vichy French colonial forces
Thailand annexed some territories in western Cambodia and northern Laos
Statues representing army, navy, air force, police, and civilians by Italian sculptor Corrado Feroci (Silpa Bhirasri)
Feroci reportedly called the monument 'the victory of embarrassment' due to dissatisfaction with the design
Names of 656 soldiers and civilians killed in the Franco-Thai War are inscribed on the obelisk

s: A royal palace turned hotel turned radio station turned army hospital
w: Rama V built this canal-side retreat in 1909 so he could look out over the paddy fields -- then died a few months after it was finished. Rama VI converted it to an international hotel; when that failed, it became the site of Thailand's first radio broadcast. After the 1932 coup the palace became an army clinic, now the Phramongkutklao Military Hospital, still operating around the palace's ornate throne hall. Visit on a Saturday guided tour or you don't get past the gates.
_facts: Construction began 1909; built at Rama V's behest to overlook the rice fields and paddies
Rama V died several months after the palace's completion, having only used it briefly
Rama VII converted it to an international hotel, an enterprise that failed after five years
Became the site of the first Thai radio broadcast station
After the 1932 change of government, converted to a clinic for the Royal Thai Army; now Phramongkutklao Hospital

n: Wat Saket and the Golden Mount
s: The stupa that collapsed and became an accidental mountain
w: Rama III ordered a giant chedi here, but Bangkok's soft alluvial soil couldn't take the weight -- the structure collapsed mid-build and sat for decades as a weedy mound the locals called 'the mountain,' taking it for natural. Rama IV eventually raised a small chedi on the hill; Rama V gilded it and added Buddha relics brought from Sri Lanka. Every November, devotees wind a red robe around the summit in candlelit procession. Bangkok's only hill, made by accident.
_facts: Rama III ordered a huge chedi inside Wat Saket, but the soft Bangkok soil caused it to collapse during construction
The abandoned mud-and-brick mound was overgrown for decades; locals called it 'phu khao' (mountain) thinking it natural
Rama IV began construction of a small chedi on the hill; completed early in the reign of Rama V
A relic of the Buddha was brought from Sri Lanka by Prince Pritsadang to be placed in the chedi
Annual November festival features a candlelight procession and the wrapping of a long red robe around the chedi

s: Where a future king spent 27 years as a monk, and a monk painted like Vermeer
w: In 1836, Prince Mongkut arrived here in monk's robes and stayed 27 years -- studying Western science, founding the Thammayut Nikaya order, and befriending a young monk-painter named Khrua In Khong. When Mongkut finally left to become Rama IV, he commissioned Khrua In Khong to paint the temple murals; the painter responded by abandoning flat traditional Thai compositions and introducing Western linear perspective for the first time in Siamese art. The first Thai pictures with a vanishing point.
_facts: Temple established in 1824; Prince Mongkut arrived in 1836 and became its first abbot, founding the Thammayut Nikaya order
Mongkut stayed 27 years before acceding the throne as Rama IV (King of Siam 1851-1868)
Monk-artist Khrua In Khong painted murals here introducing Western linear perspective into Thai art for the first time
Khrua In Khong is considered one of the most celebrated Thai artists, active in the 1850s-1860s under Royal patronage
Kings Bhumibol (Rama IX) and Vajiralongkorn (Rama X) both served their monkhood periods here

s: A prince's teak villa full of 4,000-year-old Bronze Age pottery
w: Five traditional Thai teak houses set around a garden -- the private home of Prince Chumbhotbongs Paribatra (1904-1959) and his wife, opened as a museum in 1952. The surprise is the lacquer pavilion, an extraordinary 17th-century gilded chamber moved here from Ayutthaya, and the Ban Chiang pottery collection: delicate whorled vessels over 4,000 years old from northeastern Thailand's Bronze Age. A princely garden with a time-machine room inside.
_facts: Originally the home of Prince Chumbhotbongs Paribatra (1904-1959) and his princess; opened as a museum in 1952
Features a group of traditional Thai teak houses with covered hallways
Houses Ban Chiang pottery over 4,000 years old from Thailand's northeast Bronze Age culture
Contains a rare 17th-century Ayutthaya lacquer pavilion moved to the site
Located on Si Ayutthaya Road, south of Victory Monument

s: Bangkok's 24-hour flower market, most alive in the darkest hours
w: Open round the clock, but the real spectacle arrives between midnight and dawn when boats and trucks unload from the provinces. By 2 a.m. the lanes are shoulder-deep in jasmine garlands, lotus buds, marigold crowns, and orchid towers on ice -- flowers going to temple offerings, to hotel lobbies, to wedding altars. The market name means 'mouth of the canal,' and its location at the old khlong junction explains why traders have landed here since Bangkok's founding.
_facts: Bangkok's primary wholesale flower market, open 24 hours
Located in Wang Burapha Phirom Subdistrict, Phra Nakhon District, on Chak Phet Road
Busiest before dawn when boats and trucks arrive from nearby provinces
Name 'Pak Khlong Talat' means 'the market on the mouth of the canal', referring to its location at Khlong Khu Mueang Doem
Described by Bangkok residents as a place of 'symbolic value'

s: The 5.5-tonne gold Buddha hiding inside a plaster one for centuries
w: In 1935, a plaster Buddha was moved here from an abandoned riverside temple. No one thought much of it. In 1955, as workers relocated the statue to a new viharn, the crane dropped it -- and the cracked plaster revealed a solid-gold image within, 5.5 tonnes of 14th-century gold hidden in stucco, thought to have been plastered over to conceal it from Burmese invaders. It had sat unrecognised in the open for at least two decades. The greatest reveal in Chinatown history.
_facts: Temple known as Wat Sam Chin ('three Chinese') until renamed in 1940
A plaster-covered Buddha statue was moved here from the abandoned Wat Phraya Krai in 1935
In 1955, the statue was accidentally dropped during relocation, cracking the outer plaster and revealing a solid-gold image within
The golden image (Phra Phuttha Maha Suwanna Patimakon) weighs 5.5 tonnes and is believed to date to the Sukhothai or early Ayutthaya period
It is believed the gold was concealed in plaster to hide it from Burmese invaders

s: Bangkok's oldest street: a Teochew alley from 1782
w: When Rama I founded Bangkok in 1782, Teochew Chinese merchants were among the first to settle, along this narrow lane they called Sampheng. Officially Soi Wanit 1 today, it remains too tight for any vehicle wider than a handcart -- a single continuous canyon of wholesale shops selling ribbons, fabrics, toys, hardware, and plastic goods in bulk. Bangkok grew into a metropolis around it; the lane barely changed. The oldest market street in the city.
_facts: Sampheng was settled during the establishment of Bangkok in 1782 by Teochew Chinese merchants
Now officially known as Soi Wanit 1
A small alleyway lined with numerous wholesale shops
The neighbourhood grew into the surrounding Chinatown area
One of Bangkok's oldest continuously traded market streets
